item.title: I just had a surgey on the 17th that went bad — 94 replies — 376.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I just had a surgey on the 17th that went bad

Open thread • Replies: 94 • Updated: 2026-04-24 10:34 UTC

OP

It was suppose to be a minor incision to fix a rare genetic anomaly in my left kidney, I woke up in the ICU and couldn't move, I was told there were complications and they had to change the surgey to a kidney bypass, which required opening my entire chest from my groin to my sternum.
I was in critical condition and nearly died a few times from my body going haywire, and the surgey scar twisting and spasming in the worst pain I ever experienced.

I'm still recovering now but still in intense pain, I got out of the hospital as soon as I was able to, because the nurses were not giving me pain medicine in fear I'd overdose so I was just sitting helpless.

The doctors who performed it and hospital gave me the lowest dose lortab they could for 4 days and sent me on my way, and ignoring my requests for something stronger as I can't even sleep.

Should I sue the hospital and doctor for this?
(This is a business related discussion)

Early replies (first 3)

62148874
>>62148853
Fuck yea. At very least someone owes you a discussion for what went wrong. Financially speaking. Theres a stat out there you know where hospital botches and deaths skyrocket a certain time each year when all the new medical students join the workforce. Best to not get sick around that time.

62148883
>>62148853
>that will be 20k in medical bills goykun

62148887
>>62148874
He said my renal vein was so crushed between my spine it was unusable so they had to use a synthetic graft to connect it to my heart to my kidney.
I'm not happy about it, I don't care so much about the scar, I'm not thrilled I'd got basically a piece of PVC in my body that only lasts 10 years typically which means I might have to have it done again.
I'm not even out of the woodwork the entire area bloated up yesterday and I haven't ate in 8 days.

He was an experienced vascular surgeon but my condition is extremely rare so there's not many surgeries on it each one being different.

Man I fucking hate hospitals. They put my cousin in a medically induced coma and let him rot because he had a positive covid test... they never did any other diagnostics, including checking if the bleeding ulcer he had gone to the doctor for and was clearly on his medical records was still bleeding (that's what he actually died from after a month long induced coma).

I made sure I was in the room with my dad every moment possible when he got hospitalized from a bad lung infection after knowing what happened to my uncle. Long story short, you did the right thing by getting the fuck out of there as soon as you could.

Of the 7 MD's who rotated through over the months, only 1 actually had an inkling of what was going on. Two indians, a mexican girl who was utterly stupid, two africans (the Nigerian was the only one competent), 1 white guy fresh out of residency, and the surgeon.
There was only one single nurse, a white man, who gave a shit about him, or could at least pretend to. Breath techs routinely fucked up, nurses routinely "forgot" pain killers and never set the saline ratio properly on the antibiotics. Lots of reminding people making a whole lot more money than I do how to do their jobs, but he got out in good health.

Was it life or death that they suddenly change the surgery from minimally invasive to dissecting you? They couldn't have postponed it, woken you up, and discussed the newly discovered complications and moving to a stint? Maybe you wouldn't have a case in that situation - though I would still question the narrative and whether he's trying to cover his ass after fucking up the original surgery, but that's some pretty serious intervention to be doing if not.

Shit like this is why i try to live a healthmaxxed life, and pray to allah for good luck (only way to level-up the luck stat).
Sure, at the end of the day, genetics will fuck you over, but at least i tried.
Godspeed anon, it is what it is, don't give up, be hardcore, and ADAPT.

This is why we need to completely overhaul our system to fully cash pay

62131658
It is still cheap right now. Don't buy my bags at 25$. You can buy them now at 1.28$. Don't FOMO in at 50$ guys.

62131731
>>62116681
>What exactly does it have that sets it apart from Solona and ETH?
Solana has a success rate associated with it's transactions (see https://solscan.io/analytics). That's kind of fucked up that there's a 10 to 20% chance your tx might not get processed, right? I've never seen a DOT tx fail. DOT doesn't even measure failed tx https://data.parity.io/parachain-stats?selection-mode=include&frequency=daily&relay-chain=polkadot&chains= because there are none. If you submit a tx it'll complete within like 6s to 30s. The longest a tx takes is when you transfer from one corechain to another. So when you bridge (use a HRMP channel) between corechains. And even that never took longer than like 40s from my personal experience. It's bizarre to me that literally up to 20% of the tx simply fail.
Another thing is that SOL literally has more "vote" transactions than anything else; https://solscan.io/analytics#txs . And vote transactions are LITERALLY validator messages to keep the network running. DOT doesn't count that as a tx at all. 60 to 80% of the tx on SOL are from validators to keep the network running. That's insane to think about that you need more tx to run the network than the users use to execute stuff. People are stupid for thinking that SOL processes 3k TPS. More than half of that is just to keep the network running. It's a literal scam number to be honest. It's just to make the normies believe that the network isn't a scam. The number of transactions is completely fake in a sense.
And again, the funny thing is that 1 core of DOT will be able to handle more TPS than the whole of SOL once either JAM or NOMT hit. Maybe even before that once the 500ms Basti blocks hit. If you combine 500ms blocks + JAM + NOMT one core of DOT will be able to probably do anywhere between 2 to 5x more than SOL as a whole and DOT has LITERALLY 100 cores and this will also probably be scaled to more sooner or later. It'll sooner or later scale to about 300 to 500 cores.

62131777
>>62116681
>>62131731
The 300-500 cores are after the network upgrades to 1000 validators from 600 and after a few more future optimizations. But DOT can then scale further by adding more relay chains. So, currently there is 1 relay chain that connects all the corechains (while they also have direct connections through the HRMP channels) and this relay chain has 600 active validators at any given time. At some point DOT could scale further by having multiple relay chains and separate validators on each relay chain and then having a "relay chain" that connects the relay chains. Sure, this would fragment liquidity in a sense but you could still transfer assets between the relay chains in probably like 30s or so... So in a sense the fragmented liquidity would be a non-problem. DOT is truly scalable while other chains simply are not. DOT covered all the problems because Gavin used to develop ETH and noticed "OMG, this won't work out" and then he left ETH in order to develop DOT. DOT is ETH done right in a sense. ETH is just BTC with smart contracts and DOT is an actual solution to all the problems that plague ETH and all the other smart contract platforms.

item.title: /sifm/ - structure and interpretation of financial markets — 34 replies — 15.6/hr
item.content:encoded:
/sifm/ - structure and interpretation of financial markets

Open thread • Replies: 34 • Updated: 2026-04-24 08:34 UTC

OP

https://docs.hypercall.xyz/insights/jpm-collar-q1-2026/


prev >>62136320

still working on this. GEX is pretty lazy heuristic if you know the JPM collar at least you know JPMs GEX on that position. I'm not sure how often SPX lands within the collar.

- https://seekingalpha.com/article/4592165-what-you-need-to-know-about-jheqx-collars-and-the-impact-on-spy
- https://spotgamma.com/jpm-morgan-collar-trade/
- https://am.jpmorgan.com/us/en/asset-management/adv/products/jpmorgan-hedged-equity-fund-i-46637k281


I'm just spitballing, the collar is just a hedge in JPMs equity portfolio, so it's not like they're gonna force SPX to stay within the collars range. if SPX lands above the collar for example, it means their equity portfolio is doing well.

Early replies (first 3)

62144585
Looking for more stuff like the JPM collar where you can actually infer dealer gamma without making assumptions, here's some Claude slop on that. This should let you work out at least some signed gamma exposure without making any assumptions.

And I think it's the COUNTERPARTY to the JPM collar that would be hedging, not JPM. The collar is the hedge for JPM.

dealer gamma / gex is just one small fry in the overall picture here I'm just trying to see what % of OI on the options chain you can actually know anything about without making the assumptions in the standard GEX model (dealers net selling calls, net buying puts).

62144657

62151036

62151048
Is getting collared by JPM some kind of fucked up sex thing or what
How much does it pay?

62151050
it'd be cool if someone wanted to vibe code some stochastic particle visualization or something where the particles are like dollars seeking equilibrium in some optimal portfolio theory governed field and there's like objects that have certain qualities like volatility, liquidity, maybe with some game theory or competitiveness against other actors in the system to see what happens. so you could oscillate the risk free return rate or something or volatility on stocks and watch what happens

62151058
>>62151048
it's a hedge JPM places every quarter on SPX, and JPM being a large institution means this hedge has some volume around those strikes. sometimes it does nothing, but sometimes the mechanics of dealer hedging around those strikes lead to mechanical responses

62151144
okay I came over from smg to post here.
>>62151050
>it'd be cool if someone wanted to vibe code some stochastic particle visualization or something where the particles are like dollars seeking equilibrium in some optimal portfolio theory governed field and there's like objects that have certain qualities like volatility, liquidity, maybe with some game theory or competitiveness against other actors in the system to see what happens. so you could oscillate the risk free return rate or something or volatility on stocks and watch what happens
sounds cool
though I imagine the thermodynamic equilibrium of this entire process could be summarized by the market cap weighted index, aka SPY
sort of like the average temperature of a bunch of steel, aluminum, plastic parts. different heating elements attached to them, different surface areas, convective flow rates. mass, heat capacity, conductivity.
you could really go into detail.

at the end, you can just take the average temperature of each metal piece in the lab. That's the price of SPY or VOO

So yes, you could do that. but understand where that ends, as the "summation of all those motions of particles"

62151163
>>62151144
>So yes, you could do that. but understand where that ends, as the "summation of all those motions of particles"
and same for tracking the motions and vibrations of molecules of gas, let's say of normal atmospheric air
different chemicals. mostly n2, some o2, co2, etc. These would be the "different companies" in this analogy
different partial pressures
you could add vents, to make flow happen
add turbulence. maybe it's laminar

or you could take the average temperature of the whole pressure vessel. That would be like SPY. actually it's really similar
cuz you're adding up the different heat capacities of the parts of gas, according to their partial pressures and etc.
and it all adds up to a final number for the temperature. And that number (price of SPY) goes up or down.

62151196
>>62151144
>though I imagine the thermodynamic equilibrium of this entire process could be summarized by the market cap weighted index, aka SPY
You're forgetting things like bonds, treasury liquidity injections, etc and other things that drive mechanical or systematic flows in the market, no?

The edge can be in predicting the fair price of SPY (average composite temperature) or the fair price of constituents (temp or particular constituent), or predicting how systematic liquidity is moving around the market between asset classes as it reacts to information.

There's also layers beyond just price, like arbitrage of the volatility of the index vs volatility of the constituents, or influence of factor model type investors, mechanical CTA driven flows, etc.

So, respectfully, I think you're attempting to simplify all this in a way that isn't actually productive but is harmful and counter to the intent here.

>>62151163
Yeah, this is just really reductive and more of boglehead approach. Which is fine, but not what this thread is about. I think it'd benefit you to look more into stuff like this >>62151036 as an example.

There are actors in the market that responsible for making SPY behave the way you assume is intrinsic, and this thread is about investigating those very actors and their underlying motives and flows to predict their next actions. I don't know what you're trying to communicate except 'the spot price of the index represents the composite price of the constituents', which is, well, already known.

62151205
>>62151163
>>62151144
In other words, the governing laws of the market are not physical in nature like your analogy (as you know) but the results of a population of actors with undisclosed incentives, and we have to reverse engineer this using assumptions like optimal portfolio theory and/or identifying signatures in order flow that indicate participation from some actor. Some also use things like GEX to approximate dealer positioning. These all serve to inform the mechanical positioning of participants in the market.

And like measurement of temperature, pressure or other signals the problem of determining latent state (in this case, fair price) from measured is apparent. The measured spot price of the index or individual stock is subject to various anomalies, and is constantly dislocated from the latent fair price. The better you can model the latent fair price, the more accurately you can price in dislocations, and to do so it's helpful to understand the governing forces that emerge from the actors participating in the system.

62151209
>>62151196
>You're forgetting things like bonds, treasury liquidity injections, etc and other things that drive mechanical or systematic flows in the market, no?
that would be a part of it then right?
draw the boundaries of the system to include this or that
if there is a heat input into your system, then it's not really a closed system
so you redraw the boundaries of the system to include the heat input
you then get an average temperature again
So bonds, liquidity injections and whatever else would be "a part of the system". And the average temperature of the system would be measured by "the price of SPY" or whatever else you want to look at.

>So, respectfully, I think you're attempting to simplify all this
That is the point. You were going really deep into complexity, and there could be a very easy simple way of summarizing everything
in other words, the price of SPY
as compared to measuring the motions and vibrations of a cloud of gas, or just taking the temperature of the whole thing.

> SPY behave the way you assume is intrinsic
I'm not really assuming anything, intrinsic or otherwise.
>'the spot price of the index represents the composite price of the constituents', which is, well, already known.
Yeah, exactly. A really simple easy way of looking at things, with a single number.
>and this thread is about investigating those very actors and their underlying motives and flows to predict their next actions
good luck my man.

62151212
>>62151205
>In other words, the governing laws of the market are not physical in nature like your analogy (as you know) but the results of a population of actors with undisclosed incentives, and we have to reverse engineer this using assumptions like optimal portfolio theory and/or identifying signatures in order flow that indicate participation from some actor.
well you could extend the analogy
a liquidity injection is like someone putting a heating element on a pressure vessel filled with gas
Will the fed raise rates, or cut rates?
Will some guy come into the lab, and just decide to heat up my pressure vessel, or cool it down?
same idea

>and we have to reverse engineer this using assumptions like optimal portfolio theory and/or identifying signatures in order flow that indicate participation from some actor. Some also use things like GEX to approximate dealer positioning. These all serve to inform the mechanical positioning of participants in the market.
something to keep in mind is, is there anyone who has actually made money off this kind of analysis? Consistently?
or do people who try this usually go broke, or don't make much money
in other words, a meta analysis of people who do this kind of analysis. If you figure out most people who try this aren't successful, then you don't need to do the analysis in question.

62151226
>>62151163
>>62151144
Apologies if I misread the intent btw, I think I did. On the first read it sort of came off as a reductionist 'akshully this is all just SPY' but on a second reading I don't think that's what you meant, you were just saying that's what (at least that portion of flow) would find equilibrium at, which would be SPY's price if your model was accurate enough.

But to be clear there are factors that influence the net flow to US equities, like bond yield and treasury balance sheet. So the 'whole pressure vessel' would include things outside of the S&P 500, as well as outside of the US equity market in general. But yeah I do think you can model a lot of this using something analogues to a thermodynamic equilibrium but with stochastic processes sprinkled on top.

62151233
>>62151209
>if there is a heat input into your system, then it's not really a closed system
It's not though, because the money supply and liquidity can be increased or decreased.

>And the average temperature of the system would be measured by "the price of SPY" or whatever else you want to look at.
I don't think SPY would necessarily be the average, it'd just be the average of the S&P 500. Not even the US equities markets.

>That is the point. You were going really deep into complexity, and there could be a very easy simple way of summarizing everything
in other words, the price of SPY
No, you're definitely not getting it if that's what you're saying. Why would I only care about that price? Determining that price accurately would also depend on accurately modeling the price of all other elements of the system. I'm not sure why you're fixated on the S&P 500 in particular, it's not a special case and its price doesn't imply the price of other members of the system.

>Yeah, exactly. A really simple easy way of looking at things, with a single number.
What good is that? Everyone knows the price of SPY.

>something to keep in mind is, is there anyone who has actually made money off this kind of analysis? Consistently?
Yes, anyone who considers macro factors as well as the author of that book or people who have read it and had it influence their trading. Also probably any hedge fund or institutional buyer.

62151237
>>62151212
>>62151209
I feel that you're completely misunderstanding the market and the value in being able to understand macro positioning or the flows of institutional and systemic capital. You seem really fixated on only valuing the present spot price of SPY, which is worthless because it's a known value to all participants in the system.

62151273
>>62151212
>something to keep in mind is, is there anyone who has actually made money off this kind of analysis? Consistently?
The only people that don't are the hordes of retail traders looking solely at charts and TA. This is such a ridiculous question. "Does knowledge of capital flow and market mechanics akshully help?" Like come on anon. You keep wanting to just have everything be distilled into SPYs spot price, but what good is that? The entire point here is developing a system that can more accurately where spot is headed. Where spot is at is irrelevant since that already happened.

There's no value in simplifying to a harmful degree, which is what you're doing by assuming SPY is everything and all can be inferred from it. If it were that simple the markets would be solved. I'm just getting big time Dunning Kruger vibes here. Knowing SPY's spot price is not an informational advantage and to come in here and be dismissive while asserting such is retarded.

62151299
>I'm not sure how often SPX lands within the collar.
Because you're reading gamma exposure and thinking it's first order delta. It's not.
Gamma is thrust and retrograde thrust. Gas and Brakes.
>The collar is the hedge for JPM.
The collar is the hedge for the downside where the puts create defined loss where otherwise loss is defined only when the fund hits zero. The calls are not a hedge. It's funding for the puts. In JPM's case they have to remain delta neutral to deliver shares if the calls are exercised. So they buy underlying for delivery. The more it goes up and to the right the more they have to buy. A positive feedback loop, and an accelerator for price action.

If you're looking at GEX you're making assumptions about resistance, pinning, and waterfalling/ladders if you break through key areas. You're thinking about the quality of the movement in given zones.

idk why I even bother though the spotgamma pieces are all easy to understand. That's also the guy that did a lot to develop less naive GEX models than 'I assume every seller is X and every buyer is Y and therefore the GEX must be Z'. But that info's harder to find good treatises on since they want to keep it proprietary.

62151312
>>62151299
>because you’re reading gamma
No, I said I don’t know how often it lands within the dollar. I meant historically. I don’t know how often SPX historically has landed within the JPM collar.

The collar is a hedge on JPMs equity fund
https://am.jpmorgan.com/content/dam/jpm-am-aem/americas/us/en/literature/brochure/BRO-HE.pdf

I don’t put much stock in GEX as I’ve said repeatedly. Maybe read the read before going off on ‘why you don’t bother. The value of knowing the JPM collar here is that you can actually know the signage on the OI without making blind assumptions like the standard GEX does. The mission on that front is to accumulate as much signed OI as you reasonably can, see the first post in the thread.

62151314
>>62151299
You are someone who just came in here to say corrective things without even reading the thread or post your replying to.

>itsallsotiresome.jpg

If you want to offer constructive input I’m here for it. I posted a GEX vid that explores the nuances and naive assumptions in the last thread from a market maker, towards the bottom of the thread. I’m not huge on naively throwing GEX around personally.

62151349
>>62151312
You don't understand the mechanics so your dismissal of the naive models or the proprietary models on your stated grounds is non sequitur.
>The collar is the hedge for JPM.
>if SPX lands above the collar for example, it means their equity portfolio is doing well.

I addressed the mechanics which you clearly don't grasp if you're writing that. They are paraphrases and reiteration of things you could have read and understood in the sources you cited, in fact my first assumption was the seeking alpha article was garbage so I read it. That's where the 'I don't know why I bother' does actually follow from because it was clear enough of a primer.

Your youtube boy makes some points. But if you can't understand something where the levers are as known as the JPM collar you aren't in a position to understand his larger points about his misgivings about 'I don't think they can model this there's too many assumptions about the market makers exposure'.
You're free to ask Claude the wrong questions and get your garbage out from your garbage in. Tickle your confirmation bias however you'd like.

-The US dollar would need to lose value

item.title: May need to declare bankrupcy due to severe schizo symptoms, advice? — 35 replies — 10.5/hr
item.content:encoded:
May need to declare bankrupcy due to severe schizo symptoms, advice?

Open thread • Replies: 35 • Updated: 2026-04-24 07:25 UTC

OP

I'm worried about losing my pets and gaming pc and gaming console, it's all I have left. Does anyone know about bankruptcy or has anyone filed bankrupcy? I need advice I don't want to pay a lawyer yet or talk to a lawyer yet especially I don't want to tell a lawyer I have pets and a gaming PC and a console.

I need to file chapter 7 bankruptcy in California because I had what doctors call a schizophrenic episode where I got visions of what I thought was God talking to me and I destroyed all my property after I maxxed out my credit cards and destroyed everything almost (including old gaming PC) and changed all the passwords on my accounts. I haven't even signed into my bank account online thing in 4+ years and haven't used my debit card in 4 years. I experienced incarceration during this time and 4 years of homelessness.

I have 2 lizards lizard worth 200-600 dollars and a old gaming PC with hand me down parts and a Nintendo switch 2. And SSI (Schizobux) money.

I don't want to lose my pets or my gaming pc or my switch 2. I am on SSI and I think the wages cannot be garnished

To keep what I have left do I just ignore the debt collectors, let them sue me and ignore them because my only income can't be garnished? I'd rather go to jail than lose my pets and my steam account and my switch 2.

It's unlikely I will get a job through what doctors call severe schizo hallucinations and delusions to pay this stuff back. The longest I've worked consistently before through the hallucination stuff is 3-4 months then quit because it got too distressing to deal with.

I can't make the minimum payments with SSI. So I need bankruptcy, my family also can't afford to pay me. I was completely non-functional schizo (didn't brush my teeth and lived in the woods or at the beach homeless for 4 years) and my life got ruined, I think what is wrong with me is a spirit fuckin with me not schizo but I can't bring up demons or spirits in court.

Early replies (first 3)

62148662
The libtards over on reddit are saying it's your choice, but if you have the energy and time filing for bankruptcy sounds like it will leave your credit cleaner after the 7 year period. The libtards are also saying there's probably free legal help you can get. Without being a lawyer I can imagine if its unregistered and relatively low value like your games and pets it's pretty unfeasible it can in any way be repossessed. Just don't declare it anywhere, it's not like expensive artwork or collectibles.

62148665
>>62148566
I am god. Listen to my advice. I have not forsaken you, my son.
You need to piss into a cup and drink it, immediately.

62148725
>>62148662
What the difference in just letting the debt go to court and declaring bankrupcy? Excuse my TLDR I don't want to vent to people IRL about this except my mom.

I don't even want to risk my pets and gaming stuff being taken because I know it's technically property even if my pets are family to me and my gaming stuff is some of the only happiness I have right now.

I read it's unlikely they'll take pets or gaming stuff but possible. That's a risk.

Working is only possible with a mananger who is tolerant of potential constant small mistakes and odd behavior if I can push through literal rape hallucinations and deities shapeshifting into the store to test me which happened before. The voices said to ask my manager if i was literally in Hell and said the voices were an angel so I felt i was supposed to even though it'd be humiliating but instead I just said mental illness symptomsand quit.

I don't want to work through what I literally confused as genuine Hell before due to angel voices. Also I'd lose medical insurance if I worked, happened before they took away my SSI due to working part time but lukcily I got it back because I ended up homeless after quitting the job.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62148754
They're not going to take your pets or gaming pc or switch 2 to pawn off to pay debt collectors OP.
You need to be honest with your lawyer if you have one. He can't do his job if you're lying to him.
Look up "bankruptcy personal property exemptions" you get something like $10k in personal property that can't be touched, and one car is another separate exemption. But it varies state by state.

>>62148725
>I read it's unlikely they'll take pets or gaming stuff but possible. That's a risk.
Then tell your lawyer that's your priority and he'll work to make sure you keep at least that, possibly at the cost of something else you have that might be valuable but you don't personally value at all (clearly).

Finally anon you need to actually take your meds. Hallucinations like that aren't fucking normal, and meds help. You need to just take them.

62148756
>>62148725
nobody wants to pawn your shit.
if you're serious about an heroing, offload as much of your stuff yourself as you can. it'll actually uplift you by keeping you busy. any money you can make feels like a tiny bit of progress. if you have any medical insurance left, try to get your meds straightened out. I felt so much better when they fixed my ADHD meds, suddenly everything was much easier. I'm still broke and going to the end but it makes the daily grind tolerable. Don't forget rollover IRAs and 401ks if you have them to put a loved one down as your beneficiary if you want to give that money. I did that but maybe take the 10% penalty if I feel like staying longer we'll see how the next fake interview goes

62148866
>>62148566
You need to start living below your means now and saving whatever you can. At least you got the schizobucks as a safety net, many people would kill to have even that. Put whatever you can save into Chainlink or something. You can survive on rice and beans for very little money if you buy them in bulk and with very little time investment if you cook them in bulk. OP

Today, some guy said to my friend
>you are lucky your parents are rich, not everyone is so lucky
My friend goes
>wtf does that even mean? How come your parents aren’t rich? Are your parents stupid? You make it seem like being rich is based on luck and not intelligence. Does that mean your parents aren’t stupid AND unlucky?
I have noticed a trend of the have-nots trying to pretend sons and daughters of rich parents “didn’t earn the money.”
Imagine thinking Barron Trump didn’t “earn his money” or Elon’s kid also.
I noticed the bottom 90% pretend it was just luckto make themselves feel better.
Like come on, even your parents aren’t not rich which means you come from bad stock.
Like okay, sure, “daddy’s money.” How come your dad is poor?
Thoughts, /biz/?

Early replies (first 3)

62144825
>>62144801
They're ngmi

62144840
>median HOUSEHOLD income
Why do they lie so much
A 22 year old in 1985 earning 20k is objectively different from a 22yo in 2023 earning 45k.

And median household income, at a time when people got married earlier is completely different from the median now where people get married later. So 22yos are actually even poorer, usually starting around 30k.

They lie so much it's no wonder people can't afford shit.

62144845
>>62144825
I swear people who say “daddy’s money” are poor. Like lets say someone is rich and has kids, would that person ever say “daddy’s money?”

62147007
>>62144976

62147036
the average American makes 115k after taxes. the average Amercian houshold takes home 250k.

62147062
>>62146960
>>OP gets advice
He's probably still waiting

62148071
>>62144801
My family isn't poor but not rich either. I make a good wagie wage of $150k/yr in my late 30s, comparable to what my dad made adjusted for inflation, but I am still a wagie. It isn't luck. Some of it is environment, some of it may even be genetics, but assuming your parents had you and raised you intentionally it's due to their actions and decisions that you are where you are and subsequently your grandparents and so on.

I did not receive any direct financial help from my parents apart from them giving me a good classical education that was homeschool when I was little and then a private high school. That is an advantage over just sticking your kids in public school as it meant I was able to have my college education mostly paid for with grants and did much better than average there which lead to good internships and a jump start on a career.

I also have parents who stayed together their whole lives. They provide a safety net I can fall back on temporarily if things go a little wrong without having to worry about being financially rekt. While I haven’t made use of this personally a couple of my siblings have and stayed with them while getting back on their feet. What this has done for me personally however is enable me to take some risks to increase my own position without worry. I'm also involved in my local church community thanks to my parents which is where I found my wife, where I found other job opportunities, and even smaller things like just being able to get a lot of items like furniture, baby stuff, etc for free, having people to split a whole cow for beef with, baby sitters, piano lessons for the kids, stuff like that. This is a type of wealth that is unquantifiable but has a marked impact on your ability to thrive and progress. So even though I didn't get a free million dollars from my parents or anything like that, the way they raised me and the environment they put me in set me up to also be solidly middle class.

62148084
>>62148071
Genetically speaking there is spousal choice, another kind of unquantifiable factor that affects not just intelligence, but also physical capabilities, looks, etc. It's not random, it's not luck, it's a choice.

I am doing the same things for my kids. They may not become ultra wealthies, but I can at least give them every advantage in becoming little chads and making it to the upper half of wagies.

62148207
>>62144801
Technically they didn’t earn but that doesn’t mean the aren’t entitled to it.
My kids will get a healthy inheritance, I think that’s one of the responsibilities of parenthood.

62148298
>>62144801
BRING OUT THE GUILLOTINE

62148321
>THATS BECAUSE POVERTY IS A CHOICE

62148369
>>62144801
Sometimes its skill, sometimes its luck, most often its a combination of both. And on top of that you need to be driven by ambition (ie. have high T levels).

62148493
>>62145262
>but also success was much more nepotism & regionally based 1-2 generations ago
The location part is a huge factor that very few people talk about nowadays. Back in the day there was very little chance to leave you area if you were poor and needed to help your parents around and no internet meant that all the wealth was built up on specific cities depending on the field of work instead of being spread around a bit like nowadays.
But really another huge factor is that most people in the past didn't try to get wealthy because of a combination of religion and not needing to, since life was so easy mode that you could live very comfortably unless you were dirt poor.
There was very little reason to try and make it past the average and, even though it was easier than now at the right places (in part because location gatekept workforce competition as mentioned before), it still required a lot of effort unless you were a nepo baby, so most just found a level where they were comfortable and chilled there.
Now the entire middle class is getting squeezed out, so you either make it or fall down, and people have become obsessed about monetary wealth to the point of mental illness because of this. The average zoomer would rather risk his current standard of living for a chance to make it into a higher class than remaining the same and watching how they get slowly pushed into a lower class than they started at.
This is why everyone is gambling on stocks or prediction markets and why people go en masse into proffessions like software engineering and law where the average is kinda mid but the top 10% make crazy money, even if they don't have any actual interest on those fields of work and chances are they will make the same money as any other educated job they could have liked more.
All generations are a product of their environment when growing up, and the way the current young gens are growing up is tailor made to turn them into sociopathic gamblers.

62148782
>>62144801
Income, and deriving an "income" is largely tied to business and their desire to hire people at a 1) livable wage and 2) not hire down or eliminate positions as they have for the last 30-40 years.

Having a job does not guarantee stability or success as corporations have continuously eroded the cost of labor by a) outsourcing b) offshoring c) making 1 person responsible for 5 peoples jobs d) importing foreign labor (h1b's etc) to erode wages even further. and e) union busting to ensure that nobody in the working class can rally for better pay, working conditions, stability, overtime pay etc...

basically, for the last 40 years large corporations (usually multi-nationals) have conspired together to erode wages and destroy the working class. who are now being displaced and replaced with foreign labor, automation and robots.

62148905
I have an unrelated pet peeve of mine. I'm sick and fucking tired of millennials larping as if it's still 2015 and they're paying off student loans on a one-store apartment.

I applaud the ones that understand that they're getting old. The delusion can't last forever. It's the ones that pretend they're still rocking the boat despite having a house and a savings account on the 6 digits.

62149041
>>62144801
poor people do not understand rich people's problems and rich people do not understand poor people's problems.

there is no solution. they will always be in conflict with each other.

rich people never have or have forgotten what its like to be poor so they repeat the same regurgitated bullshit about how poor people are stupid and lazy etc.
some of it is true but much of it is also out of touch. it definitely helps to have a network of other rich people for opportunities a poor person will just never encounter in their entire lives.
access to favorable loans etc.

on the opposite you have poor people who have a bunch of prejudice about how rich people got rich in the first place and what it takes to actually stay rich and a lot of that is also out of touch.

again, there is no solution.
in general, people are too full of themselves on both sides.

62149175
>>62148905
Nigger the only thing I have six figure of is dead and yes I am still paying student loans.

62149402
>>62148905
>dis nigga over here doesn't understand arrested development from 20 years of bait and switch careers
I'm not in that boat; I have a PhD and a stable career (that pays like half of what it should), but I get what my peers are going through. Everything past 9/11 has been a downhill slide into a cratering society/civilization and constant economic downturn plastered over by the money printer. There's very few opportunities to bounce back if you get kneecapped with a layoff/accident/divorce/etc since its hard enough to get started with anything in the first place. Some of those people admittedly never tried, and they're losers; the ones that just got both barrels of reality to the face and can't muster the energy to start over I have some amount of sympathy towards

62150041
>>62144850

62150364
>>62149402
>Everything past 9/11 has been a downhill slide into a cratering society/civilization and constant economic downturn plastered over by the money printer.

>the ones that just got both barrels of reality to the face and can't muster the energy to start over I have some amount of sympathy towards

62150834

I was thinking more of a normal person but obviously all you understand is the delusions of grandure the baby boomers raised you towards but could never meet.

Yaniv the Traniv slid into the code,

They traced the trail—too late to believe—
Signed at the bottom: “Yaniv the Traniv — GG, I leave.”

>is it this at protocol level?
Yes, they can move ETH since it is the chain's native token. Moving ERC20s would be more difficult. What they can't do is impersonate the address, i.e. they cannot forge signatures and pretend to be the private key owner

>They can do things that a typical validator set can do.

To insert fake transactions and rewrite history, they'd have to go through the network to rewrite each node's history individually to recreate the blockchain. What Arbitrum did is out of the scope of what you'd expect from a validator set. So what can they do?

That's not correct. Blockchains are not immutable, they are democratic, and anything can change at the protocol level simply by changing the node software.
